title: What Is Cheminformatics?
excerpt: An introduction to cheminformatics, why chemists should care, and how computational tools can help African researchers participate in global chemical discovery.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Cheminformatics connects chemical knowledge with data, computation, and discovery.
Figure 2. Cheminformatics can reduce the cost and time required before laboratory experiments begin.
Figure 3. A beginner-friendly path into computational chemistry and cheminformatics.

content:
A Chemist’s Guide to Cheminformatics • Article 1 of 11

What Is Cheminformatics?

And Why Should Chemists Care?

A chemistry PhD student in Lagos and one in London both want to discover a new antimalarial compound. One has access to a fully equipped computational lab. The other has a laptop and a browser. This series is for both of them, especially the second.

Every Chemistry Student Learns the Laboratory. Few Learn the Digital Laboratory.

Every year, thousands of chemistry students across Africa spend countless hours mastering titrations, spectroscopy, chromatography, and laboratory techniques. They learn how molecules behave, how reactions occur, and how to analyse experimental results.

Yet many graduate into a world where chemistry is increasingly driven by data, algorithms, and computational tools.

Modern pharmaceutical companies screen millions of molecules before synthesising a single compound. Environmental scientists use machine learning models to predict toxicity without conducting animal tests. Materials scientists use computers to identify promising polymers and battery materials before entering the laboratory.

The chemistry profession is changing.

The question is no longer whether computers will become part of chemistry. The question is whether chemists will learn to use them effectively.

This is where cheminformatics comes in.

What Exactly Is Cheminformatics?

At its simplest, cheminformatics is the application of computer science, statistics, and data science to chemical problems. Think of it as the discipline that allows computers to store, analyse, compare, and learn from chemical information.

Just as bioinformatics helps scientists analyse biological data, cheminformatics helps scientists analyse chemical data.

Instead of asking:

“Can we synthesise and test every possible molecule?”

Cheminformatics asks:

“Can we use existing data and computational methods to identify the most promising molecules first?”

In other words, cheminformatics helps scientists make smarter decisions before spending time and resources in the laboratory.

Why Was Cheminformatics Created?

Consider the scale of chemistry. Researchers estimate that the number of possible drug-like molecules is astronomically large, far beyond what could ever be synthesised and tested experimentally. Traditional experimental approaches alone cannot explore this vast chemical space.

Scientists therefore needed ways to:

  • Store molecular information efficiently

  • Search chemical databases rapidly

  • Compare molecular structures at scale

  • Predict molecular properties computationally

  • Prioritise compounds for experimental testing

These needs gave rise to cheminformatics. Today, it sits at the intersection of chemistry, mathematics, statistics, and computer science.

Where Is Cheminformatics Used?

Many people associate cheminformatics exclusively with drug discovery, but its applications extend far beyond pharmaceuticals.

Drug Discovery

Researchers use computational models to predict biological activity, toxicity, and drug-likeness before laboratory testing, dramatically reducing the time and cost of early-stage research.

Environmental Chemistry

Scientists predict the environmental fate and toxicity of pollutants and industrial chemicals, including pesticides, plastics, and heavy metals, without requiring every compound to be tested on living organisms.

Material Science

Researchers identify promising materials for batteries, packaging, coatings, and renewable energy applications by screening computational databases before committing to synthesis.

Food and Agricultural Chemistry

Computational tools help assess the safety profiles and nutritional interactions of food additives, pesticides, and natural products.

Natural Products Research

Scientists use cheminformatics to study traditional medicinal plants, predicting bioactive compounds from structural data, an area with enormous potential across Africa and Asia.

Polymer Science

Researchers investigate relationships between polymer structure and material performance, accelerating the development of sustainable and functional materials.

All of these applications share one thing in common: they reduce cost and time before the experiment begins. For researchers working with limited resources, that matters enormously.

Why Should African Chemists Pay Attention?

This is perhaps the most important question in this entire series.

Many African universities face real challenges: limited laboratory funding, expensive instrumentation, restricted access to consumables, and irregular power supply. These are not excuses; they are structural realities that affect thousands of talented researchers every year.

With only a laptop and an internet connection, a student or researcher in Nigeria, Ghana, Kenya, or anywhere else on the continent can:

  • Access millions of chemical structures from global databases

  • Explore bioactivity of thousands of compounds

  • Build predictive machine learning models

  • Contribute to international research collaborations

  • Publish computational research that competes globally

Consider the research potential that already exists. African medicinal plants such as Morinda lucida, Azadirachta indica (neem), and Garcinia kola (bitter kola) have been used in traditional medicine for generations. Cheminformatics allows researchers to screen the bioactive compounds within these plants for antimalarial, antibacterial, or anti-inflammatory activities without synthesising every compound from scratch. This is real, publishable, high-impact research that can begin with a laptop and public databases.

Cheminformatics lowers the barrier to participation in global scientific research. That is not a small thing.

Do You Need To Be a Programmer?

The short answer is no. At least not at the beginning.

Many chemists avoid computational approaches because they believe they must first become software engineers. That is a misconception that this series aims to correct directly.

You already possess the most important requirement: you understand chemistry.

Programming is simply another laboratory tool. Just as nobody expects a first-year chemistry student to master every analytical instrument immediately, nobody expects a beginner to become an expert programmer overnight.

This goal is not to become a computer scientist. The goal is to use computational tools to solve chemical problems.

What Will You Learn in This Series?

This series is designed for complete beginners. No prior programming experience is assumed. Together, we will explore:

  • How computers represent and store molecular structures

  • How to find and use open chemical databases

  • Molecular descriptors and fingerprints, the language of computational chemistry

  • Machine learning for chemistry, step by step

  • QSAR modelling for property and toxicity prediction

  • Model validation and performance evaluation

  • Explainable AI: connecting machine learning back to chemical intuition

  • Career opportunities in cheminformatics for African researchers

By the end of this series, you will understand not only what cheminformatics is, but how to begin applying it in your own projects, research, and career.

Key Takeaways

  • Cheminformatics uses computational tools to store, analyse, and learn from chemical data.

  • It is used in drug discovery, environmental science, material research, natural products, and food chemistry

  • African researchers stand to gain particularly from computational approaches that reduce dependence on expensive laboratory infrastructure.

  • You do not need to be a programmer to get started; you need to understand chemistry.

  • This series will guide you from the very beginning, one concept at a time.

Looking Ahead

In the next article, we will explore how computers actually see molecules.

Chemists see atoms, bonds, rings, and functional groups. Computers see something very different, and understanding that difference is the first step towards mastering cheminformatics.

title: How Computers See Molecules
excerpt: A practical guide to how molecules are encoded for computers, from SMILES and InChI to why these representations matter in cheminformatics.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Molecular representations bridge chemistry and AI.
Figure 2. Rule 1: atoms are represented by their atomic symbols.
Figure 3. Rule 2: simple hydrogen atom connections are omitted.
Figure 4. Rule 3: neighboring atoms stand next to each other.
Figure 5. Rule 4: double and triple bonds in SMILES.
Figure 6. Rule 5: branches are represented by parentheses.
Figure 7. Rule 6: ring closures are represented with digits.
Figure 8. Comparison of SMILES and InChI representations.

content:
A Chemist’s Guide to Cheminformatics • Article 2 of 11

How Computers See Molecules: SMILES, InChI, and Molecular Representations

The Challenge: Humans Understand Molecules, Computers Do Not

Chemists have drawn millions of molecular structures over the last century. We can recognize many of them instantly. A computer, however, cannot tell whether a drawing represents ethanol, aspirin, or caffeine. A computer sees a molecular drawing as a collection of pixels. It does not automatically understand atoms, bonds, functional groups, or isomerism.

If computers cannot understand molecular drawings, how can they search databases containing millions of compounds or build AI models that predict chemical properties? More fundamentally, how are molecules stored in a computer at all?

Molecular Representations: The Digital Language of Chemistry

The solution is molecular representations. Molecular representations are ways of describing molecules in forms that computers can understand. They enable chemists to store molecules in a database, search and retrieve them not as images but as a machine-readable representation.

Instead of seeing molecules as drawings, computers represent them as text strings, connection tables, graphs, or numerical features. These representations form the foundation of modern cheminformatics. Without them, chemical databases, QSAR models, and AI-driven chemistry would not exist.

SMILES: A Text Language for Molecules

One of the simplest ways computers represent molecules is through text. Because computers prefer text rather than drawings, chemists developed line notations to encode molecular structures. The most widely used molecular text representation is SMILES (Simplified Molecular Input Line Entry System). SMILES represents chemical structures compactly as text strings using a simple set of rules:

In SMILES, hydrogen atoms attached to common organic atoms such as carbon are usually implied rather than written explicitly.

Why has SMILES become the dominant molecular representation?

SMILES is simple, compact, and easy for both humans and computers to use. For example, a single line of text can represent an entire molecule, making it easy to store millions of compounds efficiently in databases. Researchers can store, share, and analyze molecular information across different databases and software tools. This widespread compatibility has made SMILES the default input format for AI applications and many cheminformatics tools, including RDKit and OpenBabel.

Is SMILES Perfect?

SMILES notation is easy for humans and computers to understand and use; however, it lacks uniqueness. A molecule can have more than one SMILES string. For example, both CC(=O)O and CC(O)=O are valid SMILES representations of ethanoic acid.

Different SMILES representations of the same molecule can create duplicate entries in chemical databases, leading to inconsistencies in data management and potentially affecting machine learning workflows if the data are not standardized.

InChI: A Universal Chemical Identifier

By providing a string of characters that uniquely represents a chemical structure, InChI (International Chemical Identifier) solves the uniqueness issue found with SMILES. InChI is a standardized identifier that enables unambiguous identification of molecules across databases and software platforms. The same molecule produces the same standard InChI identifier under the same generation settings, regardless of who generates it or where it is stored.

Which Representation Should You Use?

It all depends on your primary goal:

Use SMILES when:

  • Building QSAR models
  • Using RDKit
  • Generating descriptors and fingerprints
  • Working with machine learning

Use InChI when:

  • Searching databases
  • Sharing molecular data
  • Ensuring unique identification
  • Cross-referencing compounds

Looking Ahead

Now that we know how computers store and understand molecules, we are ready to work with them directly. In the next article, we will set up our first dry lab using Python, RDKit, and Google Colab and begin manipulating molecular structures ourselves.
